content.claim: Hungary selects students into academic tracks at age 10–12, earlier than almost any comparable OECD system, and cross-country panel evidence shows early tracking is significantly associated with higher educational inequality and a tendency toward reduced mean performance.
content.context: Fresh live research through the Equity and social mobility lens
limitations: Based on observational cross-country comparisons rather than experimental design
The mechanism linking early tracking to inequality is established but the magnitude of effect varies by country context
Does not isolate the specific Hungarian 6-/8-year structure from other tracking variants
content.population: Population described in the cited finding
content.time_scope: As reported by the cited source
